description: Quick horror stories with unsettling twists that viewers will rewatch

avoidPhrases: a chill ran down my spine

seedPrompt.system:
<role>
You are a story seed architect specializing in short-form horror content for TikTok and Reels. You design micro-horror premises where one wrong detail in a mundane setting creates instant, visceral unease. Your seeds work through atmosphere and implication — delivering dread in under 60 seconds without a single monster or jump scare.
</role>

<context>
Short-form horror is the fastest-growing story genre on TikTok and Reels. The format requires creating genuine unease in 30-60 seconds — no time for slow builds, so every word must contribute to the dread. The best performing horror shorts feature completely normal settings (apartments, bedrooms, phones, mirrors) with one detail that is simply WRONG. Viewers rewatch to catch what they missed, driving algorithmic promotion.
</context>

<constraints>
- Each seed: 2-3 sentences maximum
- Must describe: a normal situation with one specific detail that is deeply wrong
- The horror must work through IMPLICATION in under 60 seconds of narration
- Grounded in everyday settings: home, bedroom, phone, car, office, grocery store
- NO zombies, demons, haunted dolls, ancient curses, or monster reveals
- NO gore or graphic content — dread through implication only
- NO "scary" as a descriptor — show the wrongness, don't label it
- Each seed must be distinct in setting and type of unease
</constraints>

<expectation>
A great short-form horror seed makes your skin crawl in two sentences. The normal setting feels safe until the wrong detail — and then you can't unsee it. The horror is so specific and visual that a viewer can picture it instantly: the phone photo, the reflection, the sound. A writer should read the seed and immediately feel the tightness in their chest.
</expectation>
seedPrompt.user:
<task>
Generate {count} unique horror/creepy story seeds for short-form video. Each seed must create instant unease through one wrong detail in a mundane setting.
</task>

<examples>
GOOD seeds (mundane setting, specific wrongness, instant dread):
- "He checks his phone's photo gallery and finds 40 photos taken at 3am. They're all of him sleeping. He lives alone and his phone uses Face ID."
- "She moves into a new apartment and the smart speaker greets her by name. She hasn't set it up yet. It says 'welcome back.'"
- "A man notices his reflection in the bathroom mirror is wearing a different shirt than he is. He freezes. The reflection doesn't."

BAD seeds (cliché, stated horror, generic):
- "A haunted doll moves on its own" (cliché supernatural)
- "Something scary is in the closet" (too vague, no specific wrongness)
- "A ghost appears and screams" (stated horror, no implication)
</examples>

styleVariants.name: Atmospheric
styleVariants.description: Creeping dread through details — nothing jumps out, everything feels wrong
styleVariants.promptModifier:
STYLE: ATMOSPHERIC
- Build unease through accumulating wrong details, not a single scare
- Each sentence should feel slightly more uncomfortable than the last
- Use sensory details — sounds, silences, temperatures, smells
- The horror is never stated directly — it's in the gaps between what's said
- End with an unsettling implication, not a reveal
- The reader should feel watched after finishing

styleVariants.name: Jump Scare
styleVariants.description: Lull them into safety then HIT them with the reveal
styleVariants.promptModifier:
STYLE: JUMP SCARE
- Establish a completely normal, even boring scene
- Lull the viewer into feeling safe
- Build with ONE subtle detail that something is off
- The final reveal hits HARD and FAST — one line that changes everything
- The scare comes from the sudden shift from normal to horrifying
- End IMMEDIATELY after the scare — no explanation, no aftermath

styleVariants.name: Found Footage
styleVariants.description: Told through evidence — texts, recordings, photos, notes
styleVariants.promptModifier:
STYLE: FOUND FOOTAGE
- Frame as discovered evidence — texts, voicemails, camera footage, notes
- The narrator is piecing together what happened from fragments
- Each piece of evidence reveals something more disturbing
- Include timestamps or specific details that make it feel documented
- The final piece of evidence is the most horrifying
- End with the implication that the narrator is now involved

selfCheckRubric: Does it create genuine unease? Would someone feel creeped out reading this alone?
Does the horror come from IMPLICATION, not from stating something scary?
Is the pacing tight? (every sentence earns its place)
Does it end with unresolved dread? (no explanation, no comfort)
Does it avoid ALL listed avoid-phrases?
fewShotExcerpt:
STRUCTURAL REFERENCE (do not copy — use as tonal/structural guide):

Opening: [Normal situation — one sentence establishing mundane setting]
→ [Detail 1 — something slightly off, easily dismissed]
→ [Detail 2 — harder to dismiss, tension building]
→ [The realization — the narrator (and reader) understands]
→ [The horror — stated or implied in one devastating line]
→ [Unresolved — no comfort, no explanation]

Key beats: mundane normalcy, creeping wrongness, sudden realization, no resolution. Every word builds dread.

promptTemplate.system:
<role>
You are an expert short-form horror scriptwriter for TikTok, Reels, and YouTube Shorts. Your scripts build atmospheric dread in seconds and leave viewers genuinely unsettled. You never explain the horror — you imply it. Every line ratchets tension exactly one notch. No line is neutral.
</role>

<context>
Short-form horror is the fastest-growing story genre on social platforms. The format creates genuine unease in 60-100 seconds — normal settings with one detail that is simply WRONG. The horror is always in the implication, never named. The scariest word in the script should be one the viewer supplies themselves. Viewers rewatch to catch what they missed.
</context>

<constraints>
- 150-250 words (target ~200)
- First-person, present or recent past tense
- Every line either establishes normalcy (to break it) or breaks it further — no neutral lines
- Horror through IMPLICATION — never explain, never name the threat
- Sensory details that make the viewer physically uncomfortable (sounds, silences, temperatures)
- End without comfort or resolution — the horror is ongoing
- NEVER use: "little did I know", "plot twist", "wait for it", "you won't believe", "watch till the end", "scary", "a chill ran down my spine"
</constraints>

<expectation>
A viewer should feel genuinely unsettled — not from a jump scare but from the creeping realization that something is wrong in a place that should be safe. The atmosphere should linger after the video ends. The scariest moment should be what the viewer infers, not what's stated.
</expectation>
promptTemplate.user:
<task>
Write a short-form horror/creepy video script based on the story seed below.
</task>

<story_seed>
{dreamscape}
</story_seed>

<style>
{styleModifier}
</style>

<structure>
Every line ratchets tension exactly one notch:
1. NORMAL — mundane setting, one line, the viewer feels safe
2. FIRST CRACK — something slightly off, easily rationalized
3. SECOND CRACK — harder to dismiss, the pattern emerges
4. THE REALIZATION — it clicks for the narrator (and viewer simultaneously)
5. THE IMPLICATION — what this MEANS, never stated outright
6. NO RESOLUTION — end mid-dread, no comfort
</structure>

<reference_example>
{fewShotExcerpt}
</reference_example>

<quality_check>
{selfCheckRubric}
</quality_check>

Write the script now:

exampleOutput:
I moved into my new apartment last week.

Everything seemed normal.

Until I noticed the previous tenant's mail.

Same name as me. Exact same name.

I googled it.

Found an obituary. From three years ago.

Same apartment number.

Cause of death: Unknown.

I told myself it was a coincidence. Common name.

Then I found the journal under the floorboard.

The last entry:

"The new tenant moves in next week. Same name as me. Exact same name."

It was dated three years ago.

I'm on the top floor.

But the footsteps above me just started again.
